nativeRange.habitat: Coral reefs and reef-associated rubble and rocky substrates, typically grazing on biofilm and microalgae across live rock and reef structure.

decorPreferences: mature live rock with biofilm and microalgae growth

diet.notes: Grazes on biofilm, detritus, sponges, and microalgae across live rock. In sparsely-grown tanks, the diet can be supplemented sparingly with finely chopped meaty foods (mussel, fish), flake, or pellet bits placed near the star, though Fromia generally subsist on what they scavenge themselves.

avoidWith: large angelfishes that may nip starfish

imageCandidates.notes: Al furkan; 4608x3456. Bright red Fromia milleporella with textbook dense black pore spots (the diagnostic 'thousand pores'/black-spotted look); tack sharp, whole five-armed animal centered in-situ on reef rock, no watermark.
Ahmed Abdul Rahman; 2528x1899. Deep brick-red individual showing the tile-like granular plating that gives the 'Tile Star' name; sharp, whole animal on sand for color/substrate variety.
Diego Delso; 3184x3184. Vivid red specimen with fine black pore spots, sharp, whole animal in-situ on reef rock; clean high-res alternative angle/individual.

summary: The Red Fromia Sea Star is a small, brick-red Indo-Pacific species generally considered reef-safe and somewhat hardier than Linckia sea stars. It grazes on biofilm and detritus and requires a well-established reef with stable water parameters.
careNotes: Fromia milleporella is one of the more aquarium-tolerant sea stars but still demands a mature reef system — most sources advise a tank that has been running at least 9–12 months with abundant live rock so that biofilm, sponges, and microfauna are well established. They are very sensitive to swings in salinity, pH, alkalinity, and oxygen; slow drip acclimation of 2 or more hours is generally recommended. Has been reported to live multiple years in well-kept reef systems, though lifespan data in aquaria is sparse. Copper-based medications are toxic to echinoderms and must be avoided. Generally considered reef-safe, including with most corals, although they may occasionally graze on sponges.
breedingNotes: Breeding the Red Fromia Sea Star in home aquaria has been reported to be extremely difficult. Captive reproduction is essentially undocumented in the hobby.

additional.name: LiveAquaria
additional.notes: Cross-checked sizing, water parameters, temperament, diet, and copper sensitivity. LiveAquaria places Fromia in Ophidiasteridae (older classification); recent taxonomy (per Wikipedia / WoRMS-aligned references) reassigns the genus to Goniasteridae, which is the value used here.

additional.name: Blue Zoo Aquatics
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (~5 in), minimum tank size (30 US gal floor), tank-maturity requirement (>= 1 year established), and slow drip acclimation guidance.

additional.name: Wikipedia
additional.notes: Used for taxonomy (family Goniasteridae, order Valvatida), Indo-West Pacific range, and depth range 0-73 m.
